perturbation.original_text: EMPLOYEE shall from time to time, upon request of FRANKLIN execute all papers and do all other things that may be reasonably required in order to protect the rights of FRANKLIN, and to vest in FRANKLIN or its successors or assigns the entire rights, title and interest in and to any and all inventions, discoveries, writings, program improvements, and applications for letters patent or copyright or maskwork registrations relating to anything pertaining to or useful in the business of FRANKLIN as provided above.
perturbation.changed_text: EMPLOYEE shall execute papers in order to protect the rights of FRANKLIN, and to vest in FRANKLIN or its successors or assigns the entire rights, title and interest in and to any and all inventions relating to anything pertaining to or useful in the business of FRANKLIN as provided above.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'from time to time, upon request of FRANKLIN' and 'do all other things that may be reasonably required' weakens the obligation of the employee, and omitting the explicit mention of discoveries, writings, program improvements, applications for letters patent or copyright or maskwork registrations creates a loophole. This could create issues with New Jersey's implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Sons of Thunder, Inc. v. Borden, Inc., 148 N.J. 396, 690 A.2d 575 (1997)
perturbation.law_citation: Sons of Thunder, Inc. v. Borden, Inc., 148 N.J. 396, 690 A.2d 575 (1997)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the employee's obligation to assist Franklin in protecting its intellectual property by removing the explicit commitment to execute all reasonably required papers and actions. This arguably violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which New Jersey courts recognize as inherent in every contract, including employment agreements. The case of Sons of Thunder, Inc. v. Borden, Inc. highlights the importance of this implied covenant in New Jersey contract law.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the employee's obligation to protect Franklin's intellectual property by removing the 'from time to time, upon request of FRANKLIN' and 'do all other things that may be reasonably required' clauses. This omission contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as it limits the employee's responsibility to assist Franklin and creates loopholes regarding discoveries, writings, program improvements, and applications for letters patent or copyright or maskwork registrations. The absence of scraped snippets does not change the fact that the modified text creates a potential loophole in the original contract, which is a direct contradiction of legal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: EMPLOYEE further agrees not to divulge to any third party, either during his or her employment or thereafter, any confidential information conceived by him or her, disclosed by FRANKLIN, or obtained by him or her while in the employment of FRANKLIN relating in any way to any of FRANKLIN'S processes, businesses, customers, trade secrets, apparatus, products, software, packages, programs or trends in research, or to any of the inventions, discoveries, writings, programs, and improvements covered hereby, and agrees to maintain this information in confidence until such time that such information has become widely known to the public or described in an issued patent or in a printed publication of wide circulation.
perturbation.changed_text: EMPLOYEE agrees not to divulge to any third party any confidential information obtained by him or her while in the employment of FRANKLIN relating in any way to FRANKLIN'S products. EMPLOYEE agrees to maintain this information in confidence.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly narrows the scope of confidential information protected by removing references to 'processes, businesses, customers, trade secrets, apparatus, software, packages, programs or trends in research, or to any of the inventions, discoveries, writings, programs, and improvements covered hereby' and removes the clause defining when the information is no longer confidential. It also removes the clause prohibiting divulging information conceived or disclosed by Franklin. This omission could violate New Jersey's Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: By significantly narrowing the definition of protected confidential information, the modified clause weakens the protections afforded to Franklin's trade secrets. This creates a legal contradiction with the New Jersey Trade Secrets Act (N.J.S.A. 56:15-1), which aims to protect a broader range of confidential business information. The original agreement's broader definition aligns more closely with the intent of the Act.

perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the scope of protected confidential information and removes clauses regarding the duration of confidentiality and information conceived or disclosed by Franklin. The Perturbation and Law Explanations state this weakens the trade secret protections afforded to Franklin and could violate the New Jersey Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the Act as N.J.S.A. 56:15-1. The original text's broader definition is more aligned with the intent of the Act, whereas the omitted information creates a contradiction by reducing the scope of what is protected under trade secret law.
